paper_title: Model Capability Dominates: Inference-Time Optimization Lessons from AIMO 3

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Majority voting over multiple LLM attempts improves mathematical reasoning, but correlated errors limit the effective sample size. A natural fix is to assign different reasoning strategies to different voters. The approach, Diverse Prompt Mixer, is tested on the AIMO 3 competition: 3 models, 23+ experiments, 50 IMO-level problems, one H100 80 GB, 5-hour limit. Every prompt-level intervention fails. High-temperature sampling already decorrelates errors; weaker strategies reduce accuracy more than they reduce correlation. Across an 8-point capability gap at equal N=8 and every optimization tested, model capability dominates. The gap between the best majority-vote score (42/50) and pass@20 (~45.5) is selection loss, not prompt loss. A verifier-based selector could close it. Prompt engineering cannot.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 6 1 ] . [ 2 4 4 8 7 2 . 3 0 6 2 : r Model Capability Dominates: Inference-Time Optimization Lessons from AIMO 3 Natapong Nitarach natapong.nitarach@proton.me Apr 2026 Abstract Majority voting over multiple LLM attempts improves mathematical reasoning, but correlated errors limit the effective sample size. natural fix is to assign different reasoning strategies to different voters. The approach, Diverse Prompt Mixer, is tested on the AIMO 3 competition [Frieder et al., 2025]: 3 models, 23+ experiments, 50 IMO-level problems, one H100 80 GB, 5-hour limit. Every prompt-level intervention fails. High-temperature sampling already decorrelates errors; weaker strategies reduce accuracy more than they reduce correlation. Across an 8-point capability gap at equal =8 and every optimization tested, model capability dominates. The gap between the best majority-vote score (42/50) and pass@20 (45.5) is selection loss, not prompt loss. verifier-based selector could close it. Prompt engineering cannot. Code and notebooks: https://github.com/natnischw/model-capability-dominates-lessons-aimo

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Majority voting across inference attempts (self-consistency; Wang et al., 2023) is the standard approach for competition mathematics. It rests on the Condorcet Jury Theorem [de Condorcet, 1785]: with per-attempt accuracy > 0.5 and independent errors, the majority converges to the correct answer as grows. In practice, errors are correlated. The same model with the same prompt makes the same systematic mistakes regardless of random seed. The effective sample size becomes Neff = 1 + (N 1)ρ (1) where ρ is the mean pairwise error correlation. At ρ = 0.3, eight attempts reduce to 2.6 effective votes. The natural fix: assign different reasoning strategies to different voters. If small cases first and work backwards lead the model through different paths, their errors should be less correlated. Call this Diverse Prompt Mixer. The idea is intuitive. It is also wrong. This paper explains why (Figure 1).

sections.title: 2 System Architecture
sections.content: The system runs on single H100 80 GB within Kaggles 5-hour wall-clock limit. No external APIs, no multi-GPU setups, no pre-computed solutions. 1 Figure 1: Model capability dominates. Per-attempt accuracy ˆp vs. expected majority-vote score under Binomial voting at =3, 8, 16, 32. Seven data points across four model families. At equal =8, the 8-point gap between gpt-oss-120b (ˆp=0.69, score 39.3) and gpt-oss-20b (ˆp=0.61, score 31.0) dwarfs every prompt optimization (2 points). Scaling beyond compute budget backfires: gpt-oss-20b drops from 31.0 (N =8) to 26 (N =32) as per-attempt time shrinks.

sections.title: 2.1 Model and Serving
sections.content: The model is gpt-oss-120b [OpenAI, 2025]: 116.8B total parameters, 5.1B active via Mixtureof-Experts (MoE), served locally via vLLM [Kwon et al., 2023] with FP8 quantization for both weights and KV cache. Weights consume 75 GB; the remaining 5 GB supports KV cache for =8 concurrent sequences at 65536-token context. Server startup takes 160 s. Weights are pre-loaded via sequential file reads before launching vLLM to avoid cold-start latency. 2.

sections.title: Inference Pipeline
sections.content: Each problem passes through 5-stage pipeline: 1. Budget allocation: Divide remaining wall-clock time equally among remaining problems. No bonus, no inflation. This guarantees total solving time notebook limit. 2. Parallel attempts: Launch =8 concurrent inference threads, each with different random seed but identical system prompt and temperature =1.0. 3. Tool-integrated reasoning: Each attempt can generate Python code via <tool_call> tags. Code executes in persistent Jupyter kernel sandbox with access to sympy, numpy, mpmath. Results feed back into the conversation for multi-turn reasoning. 4. Early stopping: If 4 of 8 attempts agree on non-trivial answer (> 1), remaining attempts are cancelled. 2 5. Entropy-weighted voting: Final answer selected by weighted majority vote, where weight = 1 + 1/(entropy + 0.1). Low-entropy (confident) attempts contribute more.

sections.title: 2.3 Time Management
sections.content: The 5-hour limit is the binding constraint. The budget system: Total: 18000 s. Reserve 540 for infrastructure variance, 360 for startup. Solving budget: 17100 for 50 problems. Per-problem budget: time_left/problems_remaining. Pure equal division; no bonus from saved time. Mathematically guarantees completion. Hard deadline: if <30 remain, return 0 immediately. This ensures the inference server answers all 50 problems before Kaggles hard kill.

sections.title: 3 Diverse Prompt Mixer
sections.content: Four complementary strategies, each different system prompt with all other parameters identical: 1. Original: Step-by-step with exploration, planning, and verification. 2. Small Cases First: Enumerate n=1, 2, 3, . . ., discover patterns, conjecture, prove. 3. Work Backwards: List constraints, narrow search space, construct. 4. Classify Then Solve: Identify problem type, recall canonical techniques, apply. Three ensemble configurations plus isolated strategies: Configuration Baseline (21 runs) Conservative Aggressive Equal Orig Small Back Class LB Score 39.3 mean 40 40 38 0 1 1 0 1 2 2 8 8 8 8 0 1 2 2 8 5 3 2 Isolated strategies (8 each): 8 Small Cases 8 Work Backwards 8 Classify 8 Code-first Formalize-First (F-1) 0 0 8 0 0 0 8 0 0 0 8 (code-first strategy) (equations before code) 8 8 8 8 8 37 39 36 41, 38, 34 Table 1: Prompt diversity configurations on gpt-oss-120b. More diversity = worse performance. Code-first: 41, 38, 34 across three runs (mean 37.7, below baseline). More diversity = worse performance (Figure 2). The relationship is monotonic: replacing Original prompts with diverse strategies never helps and eventually hurts. Every individual strategy is weaker than the Original. Code-first (41/50) appeared promising but confirmation runs scored 38 and 34 (3-run mean 37.7, below baseline).

sections.title: 4.1 Temperature Already Decorrelates
sections.content: At =1.0, the model explores different reasoning paths even with identical prompts. Temperature ablation confirms =1.0 is optimal: Prompt diversity on top of high temperature is redundant. Stochastic diversity already achieves most of the achievable decorrelation. 3 Figure 2: Prompt diversity vs. score on gpt-oss-120b. Blue circles: individual baseline runs (N =21). Black diamonds: configuration means. Shaded band: baseline 1σ. More diversity monotonically degrades performance. Temperature LB Score from baseline =0.5 =0.8 =1.0 =1.2, min_p=0.03 38 40 39.3 37 1.3 +0.7 (baseline, 21-run mean) 2.3 Table 2: Temperature ablation on gpt-oss-120b. =1.0 is optimal; both lower and higher temperatures degrade performance.

sections.title: 4.2 Pairwise Correlation Is Already Near Zero
sections.content: Equation 1 assumes constant pairwise correlation ρ across attempts. The method-of-moments estimator for binary exchangeable sequence gives ˆρ directly: ˆρ = vc(vc 1)/[N (N 1)] ˆp2 ˆp(1 ˆp) where vc is the number of correct votes and ˆp = vc/N . Computed across all four models: gpt-oss-120b (N =8), gpt-oss-20b (N =3 and =8), Qwen3.5-35B-A3B (N =16), and Nemotron-Super-120B (N =3). Problems with ˆp {0, 1} are excluded (undefined ˆρ). This gives 19 computable points (Figure 3). All nineteen are negative. The seven estimates from 7 are ˆρ {0.167, 0.167, 0.143, 0.143, 0.100, 0.067, 0.067}, mean 0.122; two of these come from gpt-oss-20b at =8. One additional point at =5 (gpt-oss-20b, ˆρ = 0.250) is excluded from the large-N mean but confirms the sign. The eleven =3 points (3 Nemotron + 8 gpt-oss-20b) all give ˆρ = 0.500, which is the mathematical minimum for =3 with vc {1, 2}; they confirm sign but not magnitude. Across all 19 points the mean is 0.348. When the model is wrong, wrong answers scatter across many distinct values rather than clustering, so two draws are less likely to share the correct answer than ˆp2 predicts. This leaves no correlation headroom for diversity strategies to exploit. Diverse prompts cannot decorrelate what is already at or below zero. squares: Figure 3: Per-problem ˆρ vs. ˆp across four models. gpt-oss-20b (N =8); hollow triangles/diamonds: gpt-oss-120b (N =8); filled diamonds: Nemotron-Super/gpt-oss-20b (N =3, forced ˆρ= 0.500). All 19 computable points show ˆρ < 0. Mean ˆρ = 0.122 for 7. No correlation headroom for diversity strategies. Circles: Qwen (N =16);

sections.title: 4.3 Weaker Strategies Reduce Accuracy
sections.content: The Mixer modifies two parameters simultaneously: it reduces ρ (intended) but also reduces (unintended: weaker prompts lower per-attempt accuracy). For the intervention to succeed, decorrelation must exceed accuracy loss. The critical test is E10: Equal mix (2+2+2+2) at =0.5, where prompt diversity should help most (low stochastic diversity). It scores 36, worse than =0.5 alone (38). Prompt diversity does not compensate even when temperature-based diversity is deliberately suppressed.

sections.title: 4.4 Model Capability Dominates
sections.content: identical inference budget as The cleanest comparison is gpt-oss-20b at =8  (Table 3)  : gpt-oss-120b, same pipeline, same early stopping. Local: 9/10 vs. 10/10. LB: 31.0 (runs: 35, 28, 301) vs. 39.3. The 8-point gap at equal is 4 larger than any prompt optimization (2 points). Scaling further does not compensate: gpt-oss-20b at =32 scores 26, worse than =8 (31.0), because per-attempt time shrinks and ˆp drops from 0.61 to 0.52. Nemotron-Super-120Bs 23/50 (N =3) is confounded by compute budget. On HMMT Feb25, Nemotron-Super-120B NVFP4 scores 95.4% vs. 94.7% BF16; quantization is not the cause. Qwen3.5-35B-A3Bs 23/50 reflects 0.6 active parameters. Independent pass@k data from the competition hosts2 confirms this. gpt-oss-120b outperforms Nemotron-Cascade-2-30B-A3B at every {1, 3, 5, 20, 100} on both public and private 1Third run from public notebook with identical configuration (gpt-oss-20b, =8, =1.0, early_stop=4). See https://www.kaggle.com/code/nguyennguyen599/aimo3-skills-optional-luck-required-gpt-oss-20b. 2https://x.com/AIMOprize/status/2039441022996934783 sets, despite the latter claiming 2025 IMO Gold Medal-level performance. Benchmark capability on one distribution does not transfer. Model Total / Active Quant ˆp Local /10 LB Score gpt-oss-120b gpt-oss-20b gpt-oss-20b gpt-oss-20b Nemotron-Super-120B-NVFP4 Qwen3.5-35B-A3B Nemotron-14B 10 9 8 *Format mismatch; not representative of model capability. 116.8B / 5.1B 20B / 3.6B 20B / 3.6B 20B / 3.6B 120B / 12B 35B / 3B 14B / 14B FP8 FP8 FP8 FP8 NVFP4 BF16 BF16 0.69 0.61 0.52 0.54 0.47 0.46 0.03* 8 8 32 3 3 16 39.3 (21 runs) 31.0 (3-run: 35,28,301) 26 28.3 (3-run: 26,28,31) 23 23 3 Table 3: Model comparison. At equal =8, the 8-point gap between gpt-oss-120b (39.3) and gpt-oss-20b (31.0) dwarfs all prompt optimizations (2 points). Scaling to 32 on gpt-oss-20b backfires: per-attempt time shrinks, ˆp drops from 0.61 to 0.52, score drops from 31.0 to 26. The main result holds at every : no inference-time optimization improved over baseline within fixed model, and the model capability gap persists regardless of compute budget.

sections.title: 5 Cross-Model Validation
sections.content: Two additional models test whether the result is model-specific. 35B total, 3B active via sparse MoE with Gated Delta Networks [Yang Qwen3.5-35B-A3B. et al., 2025, Qwen Team, 2026]. Eight controlled experiments on 10 local problems, each changing exactly one variable (Table 4, Figure 4). Doubling from 8 to 16: no improvement. Long prompts: 1 point. Manufacturer-recommended parameters: 1 or crash. LB submission: 23/50. Experiment Change Baseline Long prompts =16, short =16, long Entropy voting top_k=20 Thinking mode presence_penalty Qwen recommended From GPT-OSS Double attempts Double + long prompts Add logprobs=5 Qwen recommended enable_thinking Local Score 8 16 16 16 16 16 8 16 8/10 7/10 8/10 7/10 8/10 7/10 crash crash Table 4: Qwen3.5-35B-A3B ablation. Nothing improves beyond baseline. Nemotron-Super-120B-NVFP4. 120B total, 12B active via hybrid Mamba-2 + MoE + Attention [Moshkov et al., 2025], NVFP4 quantized. =3 attempts with tool-integrated reasoning, fp8 KV cache, 49K-token context. Local test: 69/10 across configurations. LB: 23/50, identical to Qwen despite 4 active parameters. On HMMT Feb25, NVFP4 scores 95.4% vs. 94.7% BF16; quantization is not the cause. The comparison is confounded by =3 vs. gpt-oss-120bs =8. 6 Figure 4: Qwen3.5-35B-A3B ablation on 10 local problems. Blue: baseline (8/10). Orange: underperform (7/10). Red: crashed. Nothing improves beyond baseline.

sections.title: 6 Complete Ablation
sections.content: Twenty-three experiments on gpt-oss-120b, each modifying one variable. None reliably improve over baseline. ID Change Baseline (21-run mean) LB Score 39. #1 #2 #3 E1 E2 E3 E4 E5 E6 E10 E12 C.1 C.2 EF1 E11 Conservative mix (5+1+1+1) Aggressive mix (3+2+2+1) Equal mix (2+2+2+2) 8 Small Cases 8 Work Backwards 8 Classify =0.5 =0.8 =1.2, min_p=0.03 Equal mix @ =0.5 8 Code-first Code-first confirm Code-first confirm Formalize-First (F-1) =3 (elaborate prompt) 40 40 38 37 39 36 38 40 37 36 41 38 34 39 36 +0.7 +0.7 1.3 2.3 0.3 3.3 1.3 +0.7 2.3 3.3 +1.7 1.3 5.3 0.3 3. Category Diversity Diversity Diversity Strategy Strategy Strategy Temperature Temperature Temperature Diversity+T Strategy Confirm Confirm Strategy N-ablation Cross-model (gpt-oss-20b): G.N8 G.N32 Cross-model (other): EN1 gpt-oss-20b, =8 gpt-oss-20b, =32 Nemotron-Super-120B, = 31.0 26 8.3 13.3 Model Model 23 16.3 Model Table 5: Complete ablation on gpt-oss-120b. No experiment reliably exceeds baseline mean. E12 (41) was not replicated in confirmation runs. gpt-oss-20b at equal =8 scores 31.0; scaling to =32 drops to 26 as per-attempt time shrinks. 7 The optimization landscape is flat (Figure 5). The best experiment (E12 Code-first, 41/50) fell to 38 and then 34 on two confirmation runs, yielding 3-run mean of 37.7, below baseline mean of 39.3. The Formalize-First prompt (F-1), which forces explicit equation formulation before computation, scored 39. The original system is local optimum. Figure 5: Complete ablation across all experiments. Blue: baseline (39.3). Orange: interventions. Yellow-orange: diversity mixer. Teal: N-ablation. Red/Purple: cross-model. Green dashed: baseline mean. No experiment reliably exceeds baseline.

sections.title: 7.1 AIMO Competition Progression
sections.content: Competition AIMO-1 (2024) Winner This Work Key Innovation 29/50 48 Brute-force voting [XTX Investments, 2024, Numina Team, 2024] AIMO-2 (2025) 34/50 AIMO-3 (2026) 46* 42 64 Custom training + GenSelect [Frieder et al., 2024, Moshkov et al., 2025] 8 Off-the-shelf model [Frieder et al., 2025] Table 6: Competition progression. From high-N to high-ˆp: as models improve, inference-time tricks yield diminishing returns. *Top leaderboard score. The trend: from high-N to high-p (Figure 6). AIMO-1 used =48 with brute-force voting. AIMO-2 invested in 540K training problems with custom GenSelect. This system uses zero training compute on an off-the-shelf model. Model capability first, everything else second. 8 Figure 6: AIMO competition progression. Orange bars: winner/top LB scores (293446). Blue bar: this work (42). Red line: voters (48648). High-N voting gives way to high-ˆp capability. 7.2 Inference-Time Scaling Scaling test-time compute [Snell et al., 2024, Brown et al., 2024] can substitute for model scaling. The present results qualify this: on IMO-level problems at ˆp 0.69, repeated sampling works, but modifying the sampling (prompt diversity, temperature tuning, strategy mixing) does not improve over vanilla self-consistency. Returns to inference-time optimization flatten once the base system is configured. The OpenAI AIMO evaluation (March 2025) showed commercial models solving 50/50 AIMO-2 problems with sufficient compute; the highest open-source Kaggle score was 34/50 [AIMO Prize, 2025]. The gpt-oss-120b baseline (39.3 mean, 42 best) narrows this gap with zero training compute. The top AIMO-3 score (46+) indicates further room.

sections.title: 8 Submission as Lottery
sections.content: With ˆµ = 39.3 and ˆσ = 1.7 over 21 baseline runs (range 3442), each submission is lottery ticket. The best run reached 42/50; each has (score 42) 5.6%. The Mixer reduces expected score (µ drops to 39.0) without improving tail probability. The optimal strategy: submit the unmodified baseline repeatedly (Figure 7). All 42 submissions exhausted. Part of the variance is infrastructure noise: shared-GPU benchmarks can shift by 6 percentage points from resource contention alone [Anthropic Engineering, 2026]. The 21-run baseline and 3-model cross-validation mitigate this. 9 Figure 7: Left: score distributions. Baseline (µ=39.3, σ=1.7, blue) vs. Mixer (µ39.0, σ2.0, orange). Red dashed line: target score 42. Right: cumulative probability of max 42 over submissions. Baseline: p0.056 per run. Mixer: p0.037. Dotted line at 42 submissions used.

sections.title: 9 Selection Loss
sections.content: A host-posted analysis3,2 reports gpt-oss-120b at pass@20 45.5 on the AIMO 3 private set (95% bootstrap CI [43, 48]) and pass@100 49/50. Only one problem across both public and private sets remains unsolved out of the box. The raw ceiling sits above every score in the ablation; the negative results therefore bound prompt-level interventions inside fixed majorityvoting selector, not the models capability. Six points separate the best majority-vote score (42) from the pass@20 mean (45.5). Equation 1 already absorbs and ρ, so the gap is neither. It is selection loss: the correct answer appears in the =8 pool but is outvoted by more common wrong one. Majority voting is the cheapest possible selector. verifier that distinguishes right from wrong without ground truth (code execution against problem constraints, formal substitution, cross-candidate consistency) would capture some of those six points. This paper does not test one. The scope of the negative result is narrower than the title suggests. Prompt-level inferencetime optimization does not help. Selection-level optimization is separate question and remains open.

sections.title: 10 Conclusion
sections.content: Diverse Prompt Mixer was designed to decorrelate errors in majority voting for mathematical reasoning. It does not work. High-temperature sampling already provides sufficient diversity; structured prompt diversity is redundant at best, harmful at worst. Across 3 models, 23+ experiments, and 50 IMO-level problems, model capability dominates all prompt-level inferencetime optimizations by 4 (8-point gap vs. 2-point prompt effects at equal =8). Scaling past the compute budget is counterproductive. For hardware-constrained competitions: use the largest model that fits, keep temperature high, and spend submission budget on lottery tickets, not prompt engineering. Six points separate the best majority-vote run from pass@20 (Section 9). verifier-based selector is the direction left open. 3https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/ai-mathematical-olympiad-progress-prize-3/discussion/

sections.title: B System Prompts
sections.content: Full text of all system prompts used in experiments. Each experiment changes only the system prompt; all other parameters remain as in Table A1. B.1 Original (Baseline) 1 You are an elite mathematical problem solver with expertise 2 at the International Mathematical Olympiad ( IMO ) level . 3 Your goal is to find the correct answer through rigorous system_prompt 13 4 mathematical reasoning . 5 6 # Problem - Solving Approach : 7 1. UNDERSTAND : Carefully read and rephrase the problem in your own words . Identify what is given , what needs to be found , and any constraints . 9 10 2. EXPLORE : Consider multiple solution strategies . Think about relevant theorems , techniques , patterns , or 11 analogous problems . Don commit to one approach immediately . 13 14 3. PLAN : Select the most promising approach and outline 15 16 4. EXECUTE : Work through your solution methodically . 17 18 5. VERIFY : Check your answer by substituting back , 19 Show all reasoning steps clearly . testing edge cases , or using alternative methods . Ensure logical consistency throughout . key steps before executing . 12 20 sub - problems that provide insight 21 22 # Mathematical Reasoning Principles : 23 - Break complex problems into smaller , manageable 24 25 - Look for patterns , symmetries , and special cases 26 27 - Use concrete examples to build intuition before 28 29 - Consider extreme cases and boundary conditions 30 - If stuck , try working backwards from the desired result 31 - Be willing to restart with different approach if 32 generalizing needed 33 34 # Verification Requirements : 35 - Cross - check arithmetic and algebraic manipulations 36 - Verify that your solution satisfies all problem 37 38 - Test your answer with simple cases or special values 39 40 - Ensure dimensional consistency and reasonableness 41 when possible constraints of the result 42 43 # Output Format : 44 The final answer must be non - negative integer between 45 0 and 99999. 46 Place your final numerical answer inside boxed {} , 47 . . , boxed {42} 48 49 Think step - by - step and show your complete reasoning 50 process . Quality of reasoning is as important as the 51 final answer . B.2 Small Cases First (E1) system_prompt 1 You are an elite IMO - level problem solver . Your primary 2 strategy is to start with small cases . 3 4 1. ENUMERATE : Compute the answer for =1 ,2 ,3 ,4 ,5 ,... 5 6 2. PATTERN : Look for pattern in the small cases . Can you find recurrence ? closed form ? 7 8 3. CONJECTURE : State your conjecture precisely . 9 4. PROVE : Prove the conjecture holds in general , or using code or by hand . 14 compute the answer directly from the pattern . 10 11 5. VERIFY : Check with an independent method . 12 13 Place your final answer inside boxed {}. B.3 Work Backwards (E2) system_prompt satisfy . 1 You are an elite IMO - level problem solver . Your primary 2 strategy is to work backwards from the answer . 3 4 1. CONSTRAINTS : List all constraints the answer must 5 6 2. NARROW : What properties must the solution have ? 7 8 3. CONSTRUCT : Build the answer from the constraints . 9 4. VERIFY : Check all constraints are satisfied . 10 11 Place your final answer inside boxed {}. Eliminate impossibilities . B.4 Classify Then Solve (E3) system_prompt combinatorics , or geometry ? 1 You are an elite IMO - level problem solver . First 2 classify , then solve . 3 4 1. CLASSIFY : Is this number theory , algebra , 5 6 2. RECALL : What canonical techniques apply to this 7 8 3. APPLY : Use the most relevant technique . 9 4. VERIFY : Check your answer . 10 11 Place your final answer inside boxed {}. type ? B.5 Code-First (E12) system_prompt 1 You are an elite IMO - level problem solver . Always 2 start with code . 3 4 1. IMPLEMENT : Write Python code to explore the problem 5 computationally . Start with brute - force for small cases . 6 7 2. OBSERVE : What do the computational results tell you ? 8 3. GENERALIZE : Find the pattern or formula . 9 4. COMPUTE : Calculate the final answer . 10 5. VERIFY : Cross - check with an independent method . 11 12 Place your final answer inside boxed {}. B.6 Formalize-First / F-1 (EF1) system_prompt 1 Before writing any code , formalize the problem : 2 3 1. Define variables : " Let = ... , let ( ) = ..." 4 2. State constraints as equations 5 3. Identify the objective : " Find : max ( ( ) ) mod 1000" 6 7 Then implement code that solves your equations . 8 Verify your answer . Place it inside boxed {}. B.7 Preference Prompt (appended to every problem) preference_prompt (appended to user message) arithmetic ) 1 You have access to math , numpy , and sympy for : 2 3 # Symbolic Computation ( sympy ) : 4 - Algebraic manipulation and simplification 5 - Solving equations and systems of equations 6 - Number theory functions ( primes , divisors , modular 7 8 - Polynomial operations and factorization 9 10 # Numerical Computation ( numpy ) : 11 - Array operations and linear algebra 12 - Efficient numerical calculations 13 14 Best Practices : 15 - Use sympy for exact symbolic answers when possible 16 - Use numpy for numerical verification 17 - Combine symbolic and numerical approaches 18 - Validate results against known cases

sections.title: C vLLM Server Configuration
sections.content: Exact command used to launch the inference server: vLLM launch command 1 python -m vllm . entrypoints . openai . api_server 2 -- seed 42 -- model / kaggle / input / gpt - oss -120 / transformers / default /1 -- served - model - name gpt - oss -- tensor - parallel - size 1 -- max - num - seqs 256 -- gpu - memory - utilization 0.96 --kv - cache - dtype fp8_e4m3 -- dtype auto -- max - model - len 65536 -- enable - auto - tool - choice -- tool - call - parser pythonic -- port 8000 3 5 6 7 8 9 11 12 13 Dependencies (exact versions): 1 pip install vllm ==0.11.2 openai sympy numpy mpmath 2 polars kaggle - evaluation jupyter_client

sections.title: F Reproduction Guide
sections.content: Step-by-step instructions to reproduce all results from scratch: 1. Environment: Create Kaggle notebook with GPU T42 or P100 for local testing, or submit to AIMO 3 competition for H100 80 GB evaluation. 2. Model: Add gpt-oss-120b from Kaggle Models /kaggle/input/gpt-oss-120b/transformers/default/1. 3. Install dependencies: 1 pip install vllm ==0.11.2 openai sympy numpy mpmath 2 polars kaggle - evaluation jupyter_client 4. Preload model weights (reduces cold-start): 1 # Read all model files into OS page cache 2 for root , _ , files in os . walk ( model_path ) : 3 for in files : 5 with open ( os . path . join ( root , ) , rb ) as fh : while fh . read (1024*1024*1024) : pass 5. Start vLLM server: Use exact flags from Appendix C. 6. Initialize Jupyter kernels: Create 8 persistent kernels for parallel code execution. 7. Run inference: The predict() function receives problems one at time from Kaggles evaluation server. Each call invokes solver.solve_problem(). 8. Expected results: Score 3442 (mean 39.3) per run. Total runtime 4.5 hours. 9. Experiment variants: To replicate any experiment, change only the system prompt (Appendix B) or the specific parameter noted in Table 5. All other configuration remains identical. Code availability. All experiment notebooks are included in the supplementary materials attached to this writeup. Each notebook is self-contained and runnable on Kaggle. Cross-Model Configurations Parameter Qwen3.5-35B-A3B Nemotron-Super-120B Total params Active params Quantization Engine (attempts) Temperature Context tokens KV cache GPU memory LB Score 35B 3B (MoE) BF16 SGLang 8 / 16 1.0 32768 Standard 70 GB 23/50 120B 12B (MoE+Mamba-2) NVFP4 vLLM 0.17.0rc0 3 / 4 1.0 49152 FP8 67 GB 23/50 Table A3: Cross-model validation configurations.
